claim: The actual composition of the developed steel is Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C (wt.%) with 0.03 P and 0.03 S.
evidence_text: The final chemical composition of the developed steel has been measured using XRF (X-ray fluorescence) and OES (optical emission spectroscopy), as presented in Table 1. Table 1 Chemical composition (wt.%) of Al added medium Mn Steel: Fe Bal. 7.79 Mn 4.88 Al 0.84 Si 0.2 C 0.03 P 0.03 S

claim: Hot forging was performed to 50% thickness reduction in the temperature range of 1050–800 °C, followed by air cooling to room temperature.
evidence_text: Hot forged to 50% thickness reduction in the temperature range of 1050-800 ◦C following air cooling to room temperature.

claim: Intercritical annealing was performed at 750 °C for durations of 5, 30, 60, 120, and 180 minutes, followed by air cooling.
evidence_text: Intercritically annealed at 750 ◦C (IA750) for different duration of annealing time (i.e. 5, 30, 60, 120 and 180 minutes), followed by air cooling to room temperature.

claim: The specimen annealed for 60 minutes exhibited an ultimate tensile strength of 658.45±6 MPa and a total elongation of 12±0.95%, attributed to the enhanced TRIP effect.
evidence_text: Specimen annealed for 60 minutes, possessing optimal mechanical stability of austenite, exhibits tensile properties with an ultimate tensile stress of 658.45±6 MPa and total elongation of 12±0.95 % , attributed to enhanced TRIP effect.

claim: The density of the developed steel measured by Archimedes' principle was 6.91455 g/cc, representing approximately 11.35% reduction relative to conventional steel density of approx. 7.8 g/cc.
evidence_text: Based on Archimedes’ principle, the developed steel has a density of 6.91455 g/cc. Reduction in density (~11.35%) as compared to conventional steel (approx. 7.8g/cc) has been achieved by addition of ~5(wt.%) Al and ~1 (wt.%) of Si.

claim: The theoretical stacking fault energy of the designed Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C steel was calculated as ~35 mJ m−2, placing it within the TWIP range of 20–40 mJ m−2.
evidence_text: The theoretical SFE of the designed steel is calculated as ~35 mJ m−2. The same lies within the range of 20-40 mJ m−2.

claim: Thermo-Calc equilibrium calculations (TCFE9 database) indicated a ferrite (δ) + austenite (γ) duplex region from approximately 650 °C to 1350 °C, and the presence of BCC phase from liquid state down to room temperature.
evidence_text: Fig. 1b shows the existence of BCC phase remains in the alloys during cooling from the liquid state (>~1500 °C) to room temperature. This shows that equilibrium transformation sequence is L → L+δ → L+δ+γ → γ+δ. ... The duplex region i.e. ferrite (δ) + austenite (γ) has been measured to be in the range of ~650 ◦C to ~1350 ◦C.

claim: Room-temperature tensile tests were conducted at a strain rate of 2.67 × 10^−4 s^−1 on ASTM E8/E8M subsize specimens, with at least three specimens tested for averaging.
evidence_text: The tensile test has been carried out on INSTRON (UTM-100 kN) at room temperature using a strain rate of 2.67×10^−4 s^−1. The tensile test samples have been prepared following the ASTM E8/E8M standard for subsize specimens. ... At least three samples have been tested for minimal measurement error and average values have been reported.

claim: The developed steel has a density of 6.91455 g/cc, achieving a density reduction of approximately 11.35% compared to conventional steel (~7.8 g/cc) due to the addition of ~5 wt.% Al and ~1 wt.% Si.
evidence_text: Based on Archimedes’ principle, the developed steel has a density of 6.91455 g/cc. Reduction in density (~11.35%) as compared to conventional steel (approx. 7.8g/cc) has been achieved by addition of ~5(wt.%) Al and ~1 (wt.%) of Si [38].

claim: The volume fraction of austenite measured by Rietveld refinement using XRD data is ~26% in the cast specimen and ~29% in the hot forged specimen, with the forged sample containing ~3% more austenite.
evidence_text: The volume fraction of phases has been calculated by rietveld refinement using XRD data [14]. This shows ~26% and ~29% (volume %) of austenite in cast and forged sample, respectively. It represents that there is not much variation in austenite fraction in cast as well as hot forged specimen. However, there may be some elemental partitioning during soaking at higher temperature that may increase the stability of austenite phase. Therefore, the hot forged sample contain slightly (~3%) higher volume fraction of austenite compare to cast sample.

claim: The presence of δ-ferrite accelerates C and Mn diffusion into austenite, and a high δ-ferrite fraction (~70 vol%) may stimulate elemental partitioning during isothermal holding, increasing austenite stability.
evidence_text: Literature [3,39] suggested that the presence of δ-ferrite accelerates the C and Mn diffusion into austenite. Therefore, higher fraction of δ-ferrite ~70 (volume%) may stimulate elemental partitioning at isothermal holding and increase the stability of austenite in the developed steel.

claim: Water quenching of the hot-rolled sample from the rolling finish temperature of ~800 °C leads to the transformation of some austenite into lenticular martensite.
evidence_text: Water quenching of hot-rolled sample from rolling finish temperature (i.e. ~800◦C) leads to transformation of some parts of austenite into martensite (lenticular shape) as shown in Fig. 3b [40,41].

claim: The martensite start temperature calculated according to an empirical equation is ~350 °C, supporting the transformation of austenite to martensite upon water quenching.
evidence_text: The martensite start temperature (~350◦C) calculated according to empirical equation given in [4,42] also supports the transformation of austenite to martensite due to water quenching.

claim: In the IA750_5 Min specimen, the martensite formed in the rolled and quenched specimen starts dissolving/transforming into intercritical ferrite (α) and needle-shaped reverted austenite.
evidence_text: The martensite (previously formed in rolled and quenched specimen) has been started dissolving/transforming into intercritical ferrite (α), and needle shape reverted austenite in IA750_5 Min specimen (represented by red arrow in Fig. 4A2) [43].

claim: The needle-shaped reverted austenite in IA750_5 Min has a major axis length of 2.829±0.30 µm and minor axis width of 0.350±0.20 µm.
evidence_text: The length (major axis) and width (minor axis) of the needle shape of reverted austenite has been measured to be 2.829±0.30 µm and, 0.350±0.20 µm, respectively.

claim: In the IA750_30 Min specimen, the area fraction of reverted austenite has decreased compared to the 5 min specimen, indicating dissolution of reverted austenite into intercritical ferrite.
evidence_text: However, at 30 minutes (Fig. 4B1), the fraction (area fraction) of this reverted austenite has been decreased compare to IA750_5 Min specimen. This represents that there is dissolution of reverted austenite into intercritical ferrite (α-ferrite).

claim: In IA750_60 Min, the needle-shaped reverted austenite measures 1.42±0.16 µm in length and 0.345±0.40 µm in width, and the increasing aspect ratio leads to the formation of globular-shaped reverted austenite alongside needles.
evidence_text: The length (major axis) and width (minor axis) of the needle shape of reverted austenite has been measured to be 1.42±0.16 µm and, 0.345±0.40 µm in IA750_60 Min specimen. Moreover, the aspect ratio (width to length) of needle shape reverted austenite has been increasing as annealing time increases (Fig. 4A2, Fig. 4C2). This leads to formation of globular shaped reverted austenite, too.

claim: The IA750_120 Min specimen shows only a few needle and globular reverted austenite, and in IA750_180 Min the reverted austenite is almost completely dissolved into intercritical ferrite, leaving mostly untransformed austenite and α-ferrite.
evidence_text: Furthermore, the IA750_120 Min specimen shows few needle and globular types of reverted austenite as shown in Fig. 4D2, which has been almost completely dissolved into intercritical ferrite (α) in the IA750_180 Min specimen, resulting in the presence of mostly intercritical ferrite (α) and untransformed austenite (Fig. 4E2).

claim: EDAX analysis of IA750_180 Min reveals enrichment of Mn and C in untransformed austenite, confirming partitioning of Mn from reverted austenite.
evidence_text: The EDAX analysis of the IA750_180 Min (Fig. 4E3) specimen reveals the enrichment of Mn and C in untransformed austenite, confirming the partitioning of Mn from needle/globular reverted austenite.

claim: In IA750_120 Min, few needle and globular reverted austenite remain, and by IA750_180 Min they are almost completely dissolved into intercritical ferrite, leaving mostly intercritical ferrite and untransformed austenite.
evidence_text: the IA750_120 Min specimen shows few needle and globular types of reverted austenite as shown in Fig. 4D2, which has been almost completely dissolved into intercritical ferrite (α) in the IA750_180 Min specimen, resulting in the presence of mostly intercritical ferrite (α) and untransformed austenite (Fig. 4E2).

claim: EDAX analysis of IA750_180 Min reveals enrichment of Mn and C in untransformed austenite, confirming Mn partitioning from dissolved reverted austenite to untransformed austenite during prolonged annealing.
evidence_text: The EDAX analysis of the IA750_180 Min (Fig. 4E3) specimen reveals the enrichment of Mn and C in untransformed austenite, confirming the partitioning of Mn from needle/globular reverted austenite.

claim: Load-displacement curves in Fig. 5(A2-A3) show untransformed austenite has a maximum penetration depth of ~550 nm at 30 mN load, while martensite shows ~384 nm, indicating untransformed austenite is relatively softer.
evidence_text: The load-displacement curve (Fig. 5(A2-A3)) indicates a maximum penetration depth of ~550 nm for untransformed austenite, representing it as a relatively softer phase compared to martensite (α׳), which exhibits a penetration depth of ~384 nm under the same load condition (30 mN).

claim: Nano hardness of δ-ferrite is 4267±264 MPa in IA750_5 Min and 4150±200 MPa in IA750_60 Min, while untransformed austenite hardness does not change significantly with annealing compared to HRQ.
evidence_text: The hardness of δ-ferrite in IA750_5 Min and IA750_60 Min specimens have been measured as 4267±264 MPa and 4150±200 MPa, respectively. This shows that hardness of δ-ferrite does not vary significantly with annealing as compared to HRQ specimen. Furthermore, the nano hardness of untransformed austenite also does not change with annealing

claim: Reverted austenite in IA750_5 Min has nano hardness 3867±110 MPa, which reduces to 3478±334 MPa in IA750_60 Min, likely due to further dissolution into α-ferrite.
evidence_text: Reverted austenite region of IA750_5 Min specimen has a nano hardness of 3867±110 MPa. ... Further increases in annealing time to 60 minutes, the hardness of reverted austenite has been slightly reduced to 3478±334 MPa, may be due to further dissolution of reverted austenite into α-ferrite.

claim: IA750_60 Min specimen achieves the best combination of tensile properties with UTS 658.45±6 MPa and TE 12±0.95%, attributed to optimum stability of reverted austenite and enhanced TRIP effect.
evidence_text: IA750_60 Min specimen shows UTS of 658.45±6 MPa along with maximum TE of 12±0.95%. This could be due to the optimum stability of reverted austenite that is responsible for enhanced TRIP effect during tensile loading.

claim: IA750_5 Min shows slightly lower YS and UTS but slightly increased TE compared to HRQ, due to martensite dissolution into intercritical ferrite and formation of needle-like reverted austenite, as well as reduced dislocation density.
evidence_text: IA750_5 Min specimen shows slightly lower YS and UTS, whereas slightly increased in TE compared to hot rolled & quenched specimen. This is mainly due to the dissolution of martensite into intercritical ferrite and the needle shape of reverted austenite, as shown in Fig. 4A1. Annealing for 5 minutes reduce the dislocation density

claim: All annealed specimens show two-stage work hardening: S1 with sharp decrease due to δ-ferrite deformation, and S2 with linear decrease due to TRIP effect involving intercritical ferrite, untransformed austenite and reverted austenite.
evidence_text: Each work hardening rate curve (WHR) of annealed specimen shows the two stages (S1,S2) of deformation. The stage, S1, represents deformation of δ-ferrite thus, decreasing sharply. Whereas in stage S2, the WHR decreasing linearly as increasing true strain due to simultaneous occurrence of softening and hardening (i.e. TRIP) effect. This is mainly associated with cooperative deformation of intercritical ferrite/untransformed austenite and reverted austenite.

claim: IA750_5 Min mean WHR in stage S2 is 1100–400 MPa, IA750_30 Min is 1700–1000 MPa, IA750_60 Min is the highest at 2000–1000 MPa, and IA750_180 Min decreases to 1500–900 MPa, reflecting changes in TRIP effect and austenite stability.
evidence_text: The IA750_5 Min specimen shows mean WHR in between 1100-400 MPa. Whereas the IA750_30 Min specimen has mean WHR in between 1700-1000 MPa. ... IA750_60 Min shows the highest mean WHR in between 2000-1000 MPa. ... Further increase in annealing time to 180 min (IA750_180 Min), the mean WHR has been again decreased to 1500-900 MPa

claim: The HRQ specimen has a yield strength of 486±6 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 674±20 MPa, total elongation of 1.5±0.5%, and PSE of 1.01 GPa% (Table 3).
The IA750_60 Min specimen achieves the optimum combination of ultimate tensile strength 658.45±6 MPa, total elongation 12±0.95%, and product of strength and elongation PSE=7.9 GPa% (Table 3).
The HRQ sample contains approximately 19% retained austenite, which reduces only marginally to ~18% after tensile deformation, indicating very stable austenite.
evidence_text: The HRQ sample contains approximately 19% austenite. After tensile deformation, however, very marginal reduction to ~18% takes place.

claim: The mechanical stability parameter K for the HRQ specimen is ~3.26, calculated using Sugimoto's model, signifying higher mechanical stability of austenite.
evidence_text: The K value (~3.26) as calculated following the equation (5) denotes the higher mechanical stability of austenite.

claim: After 5 min of intercritical annealing at 750°C, the austenite volume fraction increases to ~46% (~27% reverted austenite), reduces to ~42% after tensile testing, with a transformation ratio of 8.7% and a K value of approximately 1.93.
evidence_text: The 5 minutes of annealing, however, results in the ~46% austenite volume fraction. This confirms the formation of ~27% of reverted austenite through dissolution of martensite as shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4. After tensile test the austenite fraction has been reduced to ~42%. This suggests that a portion of the austenite (reverted austenite) transforms into martensite during tensile loading, driven by the TRIP effect, as indicated by the transformation ratio of 8.7%. Here, K value decreased to approximately 1.93

claim: For the IA750_30 Min specimen, the austenite fraction decreases to ~40% and the mechanical stability parameter K is 1.10, indicating relatively higher mechanical stability.
evidence_text: Further increase in annealing time to 30 minutes, the austenite fraction has been decreased to ~40% in IA750_30 Min specimen. This supports the microstructure (Fig. 4) observation that shows dissolution of needle shape reverted austenite into intercritical ferrite. The K (1.10) value of IA750_30 Min specimen, suggested relatively higher mechanical stability.

claim: The IA750_60 Min specimen has ~35% retained austenite, a K value of ~1.92, the highest transformation ratio (~20%), and achieves the highest PSE through an enhanced TRIP effect.
evidence_text: The IA750_60 Min specimen exhibits approximately 35% austenite, indicating a further reduction in the retained austenite fraction. During this process, some of the reverted austenite transforms into a globular morphology. ... The K (~1.92) value of IA750_60 min specimen supports this claim. Further, it also presents highest transformation ratio (~20%) of austenite during tensile loading. This supports the tensile results of highest PSE in IA750_60 Min specimen through enhanced TRIP effect

claim: For IA750_120 Min and IA750_180 Min specimens, the austenite volume fraction decreases to ~30% due to dissolution of reverted austenite, resulting in lower PSE and lower TRIP effect.
evidence_text: increasing annealing time to 120 and 180 min, the austenite fraction has been again decreased to ~30%. This is mainly due to further dissolution of reverted austenite to intercritical ferrite as shown in Fig. 4D2. Hence, tensile result exhibits lower PSE in IA750_120 Min and IA750_180 Min specimen due to lower TRIP effect.

claim: In IA750_5 Min, needle-shaped reverted austenite is Mn-rich leading to high mechanical stability and low TRIP; in IA750_60 Min, very fine globular reverted austenite (average diameter 0.0185±0.010 µm) is Mn- and C-lean, providing optimum mechanical stability for enhanced TRIP effect.
evidence_text: In IA750_5 Min specimen, the needle shape reverted austenite is having Mn content similar to untransformed austenite due to lower annealing time. Moreover, the reverted austenite may lean in C, as interstitial elements require less annealing time for partitioning compare to substitutional elements [50,51]. Therefore it (IA750_5 Min) shows higher mechanical stability due to enrichment in Mn leads to low TRIP effect. ... the EDS analysis shows that these fine needle shapes of austenite are lean in Mn due to elemental partitioning of Mn and Al to adjacent untransformed austenite that further decreases its stability. In addition to that globular shape of reverted austenite are very fine (average diameter, 0.0185±0.010 µm) and lean in Mn and C due to elemental partitioning to adjacent untransformed austenite. Therefore, at 60 minutes of annealing duration, the needle and globular shape of reverted austenite has optimum mechanical stability, leads to enhanced TRIP effect.

claim: The interface between intercritical ferrite and reverted austenite in IA750_60 Min specimen contains ~65%Fe, ~18%Mn, ~11%Al, ~1.7%Si, and 2.40%C, indicating Mn and C enrichment and Fe and Al depletion at the grain boundary.
evidence_text: The interface between intercritical ferrite and reverted austenite represents the atomic concentration of ~65%Fe, ~18%Mn, ~11%Al, ~1.7%Si and 2.40%C. This suggested the enrichment of Mn and C and depletion of Fe and Al at the grain boundary.

claim: Using SEM-EDS measured reverted austenite composition (Mn 7.58±0.14 wt.%, Al 3.96±0.15 wt.%) for IA750_60 Min, the stacking fault energy (SFE) is calculated as ~27 mJ/m², lower than the equilibrium austenite SFE of ~35 mJ/m².
evidence_text: the Mn and Al content in reverted austenite by SEM-EDS analysis is measured to be 7.58±0.14 and 3.96±0.15 respectively. With these values of Mn and Al, the SFE has been calculated to be ~27 mJ/m², that is lower than the SFE of austenite with equilibrium composition (~35 mJ/m²)

claim: Fractography of tensile tested HRQ specimen shows cleavage/flat facets indicating brittle failure; IA750_5 Min shows δ-ferrite cleavage and quasi-cleavage fracture in retained austenite; reverted austenite may produce dimples representing ductile failure.
evidence_text: The fracture surface of HRQ specimen (Fig. 11a,b) exhibits mainly cleavage and/ or flat facets type of structure represents brittle mode of failure. Further in IA750_5 Min sample (Fig. 11c), δ ferrite shows the cleavage mode of fracture, whereas the retained austenite (untransformed + reverted austenite) indicated by yellow dashed line in Fig. 11c shows the quasi-cleavage mode of fracture. Similar type of fracture surface (quasi-cleavage) has been observed in other samples ... The reverted austenite may responsible for dimples in fracture surface represents ductile mode of failure.

claim: Fig. 8 schematically represents the transformation: HRQ contains martensite surrounded by untransformed austenite; after 5 min annealing at 750°C, martensite transforms to sharp needle-shaped reverted austenite and α-ferrite; with longer times, needle sharpness reduces and globular reverted austenite appears, followed by dissolution.
evidence_text: Fig. 8(a) shows presence of martensite surrounded by untransformed austenite in HRQ condition. As steel is heated to 750◦C for 5 minutes of annealing time, martensite transforms to sharp needle shaped austenite and α-ferrite adjacent to it as shown in Fig. 8(b). With increasing annealing time austenite fraction decreases as austenite stabilizing elements diffuses to untransformed austenite. ... Additionally, sharpness of the reverted austenite also reduced as some globular shaped reverted austenite can be seen in Fig. 8(c, d).

claim: For the IA750_60 Min specimen, the Mn content in reverted austenite measured by SEM-EDS is 7.58 ± 0.14 wt% and Al is 3.96 ± 0.15 wt%, yielding a stacking fault energy of approximately 27 mJ/m², which is lower than the theoretical SFE of ~35 mJ/m² calculated for the equilibrium composition.
evidence_text: For example, in case of IA750_60 Min specimen, the Mn and Al content in reverted austenite by SEM-EDS analysis is measured to be 7.58 ± 0.14 and 3.96±0.15 respectively. With these values of Mn and Al, the SFE has been calculated to be ~27 mJ/m², that is lower than the SFE of austenite with equilibrium composition (~35 mJ m⁻²) calculated initially (supplementary file).

claim: A change in carbon content of about 0.05 wt% changes the stacking fault energy by approximately 2 mJ/m² according to the Olson and Cohen thermodynamic model.
evidence_text: The change in content of C to ~0.05 wt%, change the SFE to ~2 mJ/m2 as per thermodynamic model proposed by Olson and Cohen.

claim: The IA750_60 Min specimen exhibits yield strength of 465±10 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 658.45±6 MPa, and total elongation of 12±0.95% due to an enhanced TRIP effect.
evidence_text: The specimen having optimum mechanical stability of reverted austenite (IA750_60 Min) exhibits yield stress (YS), ultimate tensile stress (UTS) and total elongation (TE) of 465±10 MPa, 658.45±6 MPa and 12±0.95 % respectively due to enhanced TRIP effect.
